title: Merlin
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00134. It encodes the first 15,556 lines of Herry Lovelich's Middle English verse Merlin from Ernst A. Kock's Parts I and II, published in 1904 and 1913 as EETS Extra Series 93 and 112. Although the XML title page names only Part I, its body continues through printed p. 416 and silently crosses into Part II at line 7,869. Kock transcribed the unique witness, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80. The encoded selection begins on f. 88v and ends within f. 146v; it does not end the medieval work. Kock's Part III (1932, EETS Original Series 185) continues through line 27,852. Parker's complete 414-canvas facsimile is cached: current ff. 197-198 are displaced leaves of the preceding Holy Grail, so the surviving Merlin text is physically represented by ff. 88v-196v and ff. 199r-200v within Parker's broader catalog component ff. 88v-200v. The manuscript and the romance both end imperfectly.
review_notes: The intellectual work is Lovelich's Middle English Merlin; CME00134 is a partial digital representation of its opening 15,556 lines, not a complete-work encoding.

The XML reproduces a Part I title page dated 1904 but continues through all of Part II, including printed pp. 209-416 and lines 7,869-15,556; the Part II title page and preliminary matter are not transcribed.

The preferred University of Michigan/Google Internet Archive scan contains both source parts and printed p. 209. The alternate Harvard/Google scan is a complete provider object but omits printed p. 209 and therefore lacks lines 7,869-7,907.

Parker catalogs Merlin broadly at ff. 88v-200v. James's collation identifies current ff. 197-198 as displaced Holy Grail leaves originally belonging after lost leaves at the beginning; current ff. 199-200 are the two surviving leaves of quire P. The target text is therefore modeled as a discontiguous current-foliation run while Parker's own broad label is retained.

DIMEV gives the unique witness as ff. 88v-196v even while recording all 27,852 lines. This conflicts with Parker's component ending at f. 200v and with James's collation; the discrepancy remains explicit.

The Parker MODS record has internally conflicting material fields ('Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum'), while its linked James catalogue record says paper with vellum guards. No unsupported single-support normalization is made.

The full manuscript object, not a crop of the Merlin leaves, is retained in the Git-ignored source cache. The work mapping points from the complete 414-canvas bundle to the CME selection at canvases 184-300.

The 15,556 body L elements match the terminal editorial number, but seven explicit L/@N values differ from their ordinal XML positions. Counts are encoding metrics rather than a silent reconstruction of Kock's lineation.

Kölbing's 1890 lines 1-1,638 and Furnivall's 1863 146-line Mordred excerpt are independently transcribed partial editions from the same witness. Neither is asserted as an immediate source of Kock's edition.

The Parker images are CC BY-NC 4.0. Their private local cache does not authorize commercial redistribution; Parker directs scholarly or commercial high-resolution publication enquiries to parker-library@corpus.cam.ac.uk.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00134
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00134; the file is 1,260,769 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00134
entities.description: A public electronic text of Kock's editorial lines 1-15,556. Its 1904 Part I title page is followed by a body that crosses the 1913 Part II boundary without a second title page, ending at printed p. 416 in the middle of Lovelich's romance.

entities.label: Merlin
alternate_labels: Now ginneth the devil to wrathen him sore
entities.description: Herry Lovelich's Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the French Vulgate Merlin, probably composed in London in the 1420s according to the Database of Middle English Romance. The unique manuscript preserves 27,852 lines in rhyming couplets but loses an unknown number of leaves at the end; the extant translation stops about halfway through its French source, and it is unknown whether Lovelich translated more.

date_statements.label: Current specialist dating
date_statements.notes: DMR gives the 1420s; Ackerman reports an argument for approximately 1425 tied to the death of Lovelich's patron Henry Barton in 1434.

date_statements.label: Traditional dating
date_statements.notes: A competing traditional work date, preserved separately from both the 1420s claim and the later physical-witness date.

notes: Extant records that the work survives; the unique witness is end-defective, and Kock's transcription is complete only relative to the surviving text.
Herry is retained as the preferred given-name form used by the edition and Ackerman; Henry is a common modern authority form.

entities.label: French Vulgate Merlin
entities.description: The French prose Arthurian romance translated and versified by Lovelich. Current scholarship identifies the Vulgate Merlin as his textual source tradition, but no surviving physical French manuscript is established as his actual exemplar.

entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part I (1904)
entities.description: The first installment of Kock's transcription of the unique manuscript: editorial lines 1-7,868, printed pp. 1-208, with chapter headings, marginal summaries, manuscript-column milestones, italic expansions, and textual footnotes.

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part I, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 93 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904), pp. 1-208, lines 1-7,868.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part II (1913)
entities.description: The second installment of Kock's transcription: editorial lines 7,869-15,556, printed pp. 209-416. CME00134 includes its entire text but not its title page and preliminary matter.

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part II, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 112 (London: Early English Text Society, 1913), pp. 209-416, lines 7,869-15,556.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.label: Ernst A. Kock, Merlin, Part III (1932)
entities.description: The final textual installment of Kock's edition, continuing the extant manuscript from line 15,557 through line 27,852 on printed pp. 417-739. It completes the printed surviving text, not the lost ending of the medieval romance. Ackerman reports that the promised broader critical apparatus was not completed.

bibliographic.citation: Herry Lovelich, Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance, ed. Ernst A. Kock, Part III, Early English Text Society, Original Series 185 (London: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1932), pp. 417-739, lines 15,557-27,852.
bibliographic.title: Merlin: A Middle-English Metrical Version of a French Romance

entities.label: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 80
entities.description: The unique manuscript of Lovelich's History of the Holy Grail and Merlin, produced in England in the fifteenth century. Parker associates Lovelich and his work with London's Skinners' Company, but that authorial context does not establish London as the manuscript's copying place. Parker broadly catalogs Merlin at ff. 88v-200v and says it ends imperfectly. James's collation shows that current ff. 197-198 are displaced Holy Grail leaves, while ff. 199-200 are the two surviving leaves of quire P; the surviving Merlin text is therefore represented in current order at ff. 88v-196v and ff. 199r-200v.

date_statements.label: James catalogue dating
date_statements.notes: James's broad date for the physical manuscript; it is not a date for Lovelich's composition event.

date_statements.label: Parker summary dating
date_statements.notes: Parker's narrower summary date for the physical witness, retained separately from James's broader fifteenth-century dating.

holding.notes: Held by the Parker Library, Corpus Christi College, Cambridge.

physical_description.support: Conflicting official descriptions: James says paper with twelfth-century vellum guards; Parker MODS includes both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum' material fields
physical_description.format: Bound codex; double columns in a current hand
notes: Double columns have approximately 65, 69, or 74 lines.
Collation: A gone; B16 wants 1-9, with B10-11 now ff.197-198 and B12-16 in situ; D16 wants 4; E16-O16; displaced B10-11; P with only leaves 1-2 surviving.
The History of the Holy Grail ends on f.88v, where Merlin begins lower on the same surface.
Current ff.197-198 preserve displaced opening Holy Grail material, not Merlin; Merlin resumes on current f.199r and ends imperfectly on f.200v.
Later fifteenth-century annotations are associated with John Cok of St Bartholomew's, Smithfield.

entities.label: Parker Library complete digital facsimile of CCCC MS 80
entities.description: The complete 414-canvas color IIIF object, from front outside binding through back outside binding. CME00134 occupies the lower part of f.88v through part of f.146v, canvases 184-300; the cached object retains the entire codex.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with 414 color canvases

entities.label: Internet Archive University of Michigan/Google scan of Kock Parts I-II
entities.description: A complete 442-page provider object reproducing Kock's Parts I and II, including the Part II title matter and printed p.209. The CME text runs from BookReader n9 to n430; printed p.209 is n223.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete image-container PDF with OCR and item metadata

entities.label: Internet Archive Harvard/Google scan of Kock Parts I-II
entities.description: A 447-page provider object reproducing the two Kock parts but omitting printed p.209 and its lines 7,869-7,907. It is complete as downloaded from the provider, but partial with respect to the source edition and CME text.
entities.reproduction_format: Image-container PDF with OCR, scandata, and item metadata

entities.label: Internet Archive restricted scan of the 1971 Kock Part III reprint
entities.description: A 338-image scan of Oxford University Press's 1971 reprint of Kock Part III. Internet Archive exposes the item record but restricts the readable derivatives to its controlled borrowing system; only public metadata was cached.
entities.reproduction_format: Restricted digital lending object; encrypted PDF/EPUB derivatives

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing, Merlin von Lonelich the Skinner, lines 1-1,638 (1890)
entities.description: A partial edition of Lovelich's opening 1,638 lines, printed as the third appendix in Kölbing's Arthour and Merlin on pp.373-408. It predates Kock and cites manuscript folios in its text and apparatus.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing, ed., Arthour and Merlin, Altenglische Bibliothek 4 (Leipzig: O. R. Reisland, 1890), appendix III, 'Merlin von Lonelich the Skinner,' pp.373-408, lines 1-1,638.
bibliographic.title: Arthour and Merlin, nach der Auchinleck-Hs. nebst zwei Beilagen

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Kölbing's Arthour and Merlin (1890)
entities.description: A complete 704-page PDF of the volume. The Lovelich appendix occupies printed pp.373-408, PDF pages and BookReader leaves 569-604.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF with OCR, metadata, and page-number map

entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, The Birthe and the Engendrure of Mordret (1863)
entities.description: A 146-line excerpt from Lovelich's Merlin, copied from CCCC MS 80 f.135, column 1, and printed with parallel French source text as an appendix to volume II of Seynt Graal or the Sank Ryal, pp.393-396.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., 'The Birthe and the Engendrure of Mordret,' in Seynt Graal, or, The Sank Ryal, vol. II (London: J. B. Nichols and Sons for the Roxburghe Club, 1863), pp.393-396.
bibliographic.title: The Birthe and the Engendrure of Mordret
bibliographic.container_title: Seynt Graal, or, The Sank Ryal
bibliographic.publisher: J. B. Nichols and Sons for the Roxburghe Club

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Furnivall's Seynt Graal volume II
entities.description: A complete 472-page PDF of the 1863 volume. The Lovelich excerpt occupies printed pp.393-396, PDF pages 449-452, and BookReader leaves n448-n451.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF with OCR and item metadata

entities.label: DIMEV record 3734 / IMEV-NIMEV 2312
entities.description: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse record for Lovelich's Merlin, identifying its title, author, 27,852 couplet lines, Arthurian-romance subjects, unique witness, and three editions or excerpts.

entities.label: Database of Middle English Romance: Merlin
entities.description: A current specialist romance record supplying date and place, couplet form, plot summaries, keywords, unique witness, extant incompleteness, French-source relationship, and modern editions.

editorial_practices.description: Kock prints many expanded letters in italic type. CME retains these sequences as HI REND='italic'; they should not be normalized as modern emphasis.
editorial_practices.scope: Kock Parts I-II and CME00134 lines 1-15,556
editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations and editorially supplied letter sequences
editorial_practices.representation: Italic type in the print; XML HI REND='italic'

editorial_practices.description: Kock surrounds the verse with modern English marginal plot summaries, manuscript-column markers, and textual footnotes. CME encodes these as marginal or foot NOTE elements and MILESTONE elements rather than intrinsic poem lines.
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial marginal summaries, folio-column markers, and footnotes

editorial_practices.description: CME's fourteen chapter DIV2 elements reproduce Kock's editorial chapter sequence across Parts I-II. They are useful navigation units but are not asserted as authorial medieval divisions.
editorial_practices.representation: Fourteen numbered chapter divisions with marginal synopses

editorial_practices.description: The encoding retains the Part I title page, omits Part II title and preliminary text, represents four intervening surfaces with unnumbered PB elements, and resumes at printed p.209. Its administrative title therefore understates the encoded carrier.
editorial_practices.scope: Boundary between editorial lines 7,868 and 7,869 / printed pp.208-209
editorial_practices.representation: One title-page paratext wrapper at (//DIV1)[1], followed by one continuous poem wrapper at (//DIV1)[2] with four unnumbered PB elements and no Part II title division; the fourteen editorial chapters are (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]

editorial_practices.description: CME ends at line 15,556 and printed p.416, while the manuscript sentence and romance continue. The encoding represents Kock Parts I-II rather than the complete 27,852-line surviving work.
editorial_practices.source_feature: Surviving Merlin continues through Kock Part III
editorial_practices.representation: 15,556 L elements ending within chapter XIV and f.146v

editorial_practices.description: The Harvard/Google scan omits printed p.209 and its lines 7,869-7,907, moving from Part II preliminary matter directly to p.210.

notes: Automated retrieval encountered a Cloudflare challenge during this audit; the stable item route and checked-in representation remain identified.

work_portion.label: CME00134-encoded opening of Lovelich's Merlin
locators: current foliation f.88v through part of f.146v
full surviving Merlin in current order continues through ff.88v-196v and ff.199r-200v
notes: Merlin begins lower on f.88v after the Holy Grail ends on the same canvas.
The CME endpoint is inside f.146v, not at the end of that surface or the medieval work.
The provider's canvas identifiers for canvases 184 and 300 return HTTP 404 when dereferenced, so the working exact image URLs are used as boundary links.
The complete manuscript is cached, not only the target leaves.

notes: A new permission or high-resolution delivery is separate from the public 1600-pixel CC BY-NC object and should be retained with its own terms.

rights.basis: The medieval work predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, modern editions, encodings, and manuscript-image rights remain separate.
The manuscript text is centuries outside copyright term; this does not govern Parker's modern photographs or physical-access conditions.

rights.component: 1904 Part I text, typography, and apparatus
rights.basis: The installment was published more than 95 years ago; Internet Archive metadata for the combined scan also records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
notes: Status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

rights.component: 1913 Part II text, typography, and apparatus
rights.basis: The installment was published more than 95 years ago; Internet Archive metadata for the combined scan also records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
notes: Status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

rights.basis: A 1932 publication can remain within the maximum United States term through the end of 2027 if statutory copyright was secured and renewed; no renewal determination was completed.
notes: The underlying medieval text remains public domain; this status concerns Kock's 1932 editorial layer.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The U-M CME collection presents its materials as public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
notes: The assertion is jurisdiction-specific and does not automatically license separate scans or Parker images.

rights.component: Modern XML markup and repository-specific contribution
rights.basis: The CME submodule's root LICENSE dedicates software to the public domain but does not expressly resolve every textual and markup layer in this item. Public-domain status of the medieval text and old source installments does not by itself decide rights in modern markup.

The exact IIIF manifest and MODS record apply Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 and direct users seeking higher-resolution scholarly or commercial publication images to the Parker Library.
notes: Attribution: Images courtesy of The Parker Library, Corpus Christi College, Cambridge.
Commercial publication is outside the public license; contact parker-library@corpus.cam.ac.uk for separate permission or suitable files.
The Git-ignored cache is a private research copy and should not be committed or redistributed as an open facsimile.

rights.basis: Internet Archive marks the source NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States, while the embedded Google usage notice requests personal non-commercial use, attribution, and no automated querying.
notes: The provider notice is preserved separately from the public-domain status of the source edition.
The cached scan is a private research aid and is not intended for repository redistribution.

rights.basis: Internet Archive marks the source NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States, while the embedded Google usage notice requests personal non-commercial use, attribution, and no automated querying.

rights.component: 1971 reprint and restricted Internet Archive lending object
rights.basis: The reprint is dated 1971, the 1932 editorial layer may remain protected, and Internet Archive restricts readable derivatives to controlled lending.
notes: Only public metadata was cached. No restricted book files were downloaded or redistributed.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1890 and is beyond the maximum United States copyright term.
The source volume is public domain in the United States, but the inspected Internet Archive metadata supplies no item-specific open license or possible-copyright-status field for the derivatives.
The volume was published in 1863 and is beyond the maximum United States copyright term.
The source volume is public domain in the United States, but the inspected item metadata supplies no item-specific open license for the scan derivatives.

rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 source data, documentation, and repository contents

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME source XML for CME00134
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; IDG; TEXT/@LANG; (//DIV1)[1] title-page paratext in FRONT; (//DIV1)[2] poem in BODY; (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]; PB, MILESTONE, LG, L, NOTE, HI, and SUP
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00134, preserves the Part I title page in the first top-level DIV1 wrapper, and encodes a continuous 15,556-line poem in the second top-level DIV1 wrapper through printed p.416 and manuscript milestone 146d.

evidence.citation: Local structural, line-number, page-boundary, and source comparison of CME00134.xml
evidence.locator: 1,260,769 bytes; 2 DIV1 overall: (//DIV1)[1] title-page paratext and (//DIV1)[2] poem; 14 chapters at (//DIV1)[2]/DIV2[1-14]; 15,556 body L, 268 LG, 234 MILESTONE, 4,222 NOTE, 421 PB, 7 P
evidence.summary: Confirms IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID; the title-page/poem wrapper distinction; fourteen editorial chapters within the poem wrapper; 15,556 nonempty body L elements; 415 unique numbered pages 2-416 plus six unnumbered PB elements; Part II's silent start after four unnumbered PBs; f.88v/f.146v manuscript boundaries; and seven explicit L/@N values that differ from ordinal XML position.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00134
evidence.summary: Official stable item route for CME00134. Automated access returned a Cloudflare challenge during the audit, so item content was verified through the pinned repository XML and source scans rather than inferred from the challenge page.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, MS 080
evidence.locator: title, summary, date, language, extent, collation, component records, and accessCondition
evidence.summary: Official holding description for MS 80: unique Lovelich Holy Grail and Merlin, separately stated fifteenth-century and c.1450-75 dates, 200 folios, broad Merlin component ff.88v-200v ending imperfectly, Lovelich's London/Skinners' Company association rather than a manuscript copying-place claim, John Cok annotations, exact image attribution, CC BY-NC 4.0, and Parker reproduction contact. Its material fields conflict internally.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of Corpus Christi College, Cambridge, MS 80 record
evidence.summary: Describes paper support with vellum guards, 200 folios, double columns, fifteenth-century current hand, dimensions and collation; identifies current ff.197-198 as displaced B10-11 containing the surviving beginning of Holy Grail, and Merlin from f.88v to an imperfect ending on f.200v.

evidence.citation: Parker Library complete IIIF manifest for CCCC MS 80
evidence.locator: 414 canvases; canvas 184 f.88v; canvas 300 f.146v; canvases 401-404 ff.197r-198v; canvases 405-408 ff.199r-200v
evidence.summary: The provider manifest enumerates the complete physical object from front outside binding to back outside binding, supplies all exact image services, applies CC BY-NC 4.0, gives attribution, and directs higher-resolution scholarly or commercial publication requests to Parker.

evidence.citation: Direct visual and inventory inspection of the complete Parker MS 80 bundle
evidence.summary: Confirms complete-object coverage, exact first and last canvases, the Merlin opening lower on f.88v, the CME endpoint on f.146v, labels and image URLs for ff.195v-200v, and visual continuity around the displaced-leaf sequence. James's collation, not handwriting appearance alone, identifies ff.197-198 as Holy Grail leaves.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Merlin
evidence.locator: date, place, form, IMEV, keywords, manuscript, survival statement, summaries, and modern editions
evidence.summary: Dates composition to the 1420s in London; identifies rhyming couplets and IMEV 2312; describes the unique CCCC MS 80 witness at ff.88v-200v; states that unknown leaves are missing at the end, the translation stops halfway through the French source, and Lovelich's original extent is unknowable; supplies plot summaries and thematic keywords.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse version 1.0, record 3734 and related data
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-3734; Manuscripts.xml CamCCC80; Bibliography.xml Kock1904-32, Furnivall1861, and Kolbing1890
evidence.summary: Identifies Henry Lovelich, Merlin, DIMEV 3734, IMEV/NIMEV 2312, 27,852 couplet lines, Arthurian literature and romance subjects, one witness at stated ff.88v-196v, Kock's three-part edition, Furnivall's 146-line f.135 excerpt, Kölbing's lines 1-1,638, and an Essex LALME/eLALME profile for MS 80. The complete data release is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, Henry Lovelich, Merlin
evidence.locator: work record, manuscript, editions, and bibliography
evidence.summary: Gives the stable title Merlin, circa-1450 date, English romance genre, 27,852-line extent, prose Roman de Merlin adaptation, unique MS 80 locus ff.88v-200v, Kock's 1904-1932 three-part edition, Furnivall's excerpt, Ackerman bibliography, and Wikidata Q135089374. Last updated 22 June 2025.

evidence.citation: Robert W. Ackerman, 'Herry Lovelich's Merlin,' PMLA 67.4 (1952): 473-484
evidence.locator: article extract and publicly exposed references/notes 5-6, 15, 18, and 33
evidence.summary: Cites Kock's three installments and says notes and other critical apparatus are lacking; quotes James's paper manuscript description; discusses conventional c.1450 versus suggested c.1425 dating tied to patron Henry Barton's death in 1434; explains the Lovelich name evidence; and reports that EETS had no plans in 1949 to complete Kock's promised apparatus.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing, Arthour and Merlin (1890), complete Internet Archive scan
evidence.locator: appendix title before printed p.373; Lovelich lines 1-1,638 on pp.373-408 / PDF pages and BookReader leaves 569-604
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies appendix III, its title 'Merlin von Lonelich the Skinner,' line range 1-1,638, manuscript folio markers, editorial notes, exact beginning and ending, and complete-volume retention.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and page-number map for arthourmerlinnac00koeluoft
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1890 Leipzig volume, complete public PDF and OCR derivatives, and 708 scanned images; the cached page-number map confirms BookReader n569 as printed p.373 and n604 as p.408. No item-specific rights field was found.
notes: Cached page-number map SHA-256: db32ada1c41e56a1499e1075551b01634e8d735a3728ad3a4aae543bfb061885. Cached OCR SHA-256: b426571bbb9c84cd6f3ac1ee922aaec5ce901ccdfc129aa662554f88bc4a4519.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Seynt Graal or the Sank Ryal, volume II (1863), complete Internet Archive scan
evidence.locator: volume II title page dated 1863; appendix pp.393-396 / PDF pages 449-452 / BookReader n448-n451
evidence.summary: Direct inspection verifies the 146-line appendix title, the statement that it comes from Lovelich's Merlin in CCCC MS 80 f.135 column 1, parallel French text, exact boundaries, and complete-volume retention.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for cu31924064990850
evidence.summary: Identifies the Cornell source copy, complete volume II scan, public PDF and OCR derivatives, appendix description, and generic 1861 series date; the inspected volume II title page establishes 1863. No item-specific open license was found.
notes: Cached OCR SHA-256: b30c80328508157d95566235f994bc7c1e0a10588cdffefeef5ac62a91957aab.

evidence.citation: Local source-cache integrity, coverage, page-range, and hard-link audit for CME00134
evidence.locator: twenty cached files: complete Parker IIIF bundle; complete Kock Parts I-II provider objects and derivatives; complete Kölbing and Furnivall volumes and derivatives; DIMEV v1.0; Parker catalog records; Part III public metadata
evidence.summary: Verified file byte counts and SHA-256 values; ZIP, JSON, XML, and PDF readability; 414-image Parker inventory and exact work canvases; 442/447/704/472 PDF page counts; Kock Part II p.209 presence or absence; partial-edition boundaries; and matching hard-link inodes between the research worktree and canonical main cache. Restricted Part III book files were not downloaded.

open_questions.question: Which physical French manuscript or textual subfamily supplied Lovelich's Vulgate Merlin exemplar?
next_steps: Consult modern textual studies and direct collations of all distinctive Lovelich readings against French Vulgate Merlin witnesses.
Do not promote a surviving French codex into the primary chain without verbal and codicological evidence.
Retain the current work-level version_of relation until an actual physical exemplar is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: How do Kock Part III's final lines map exactly across current ff.196v, the displaced Holy Grail leaves ff.197-198, and the surviving quire-P leaves ff.199-200?
next_steps: Obtain unrestricted access to Part III or inspect a physical copy and record every manuscript-column milestone from line 15,557 onward.
Collate the final printed lines directly against Parker canvases 399-408.
Reconcile DIMEV's ff.88v-196v endpoint with Parker's f.200v component endpoint without treating displaced ff.197-198 as Merlin.

open_questions.question: Why does Parker's MODS record contain both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum' material fields when James describes a paper codex with vellum guards?
next_steps: Ask the Parker Library whether the vellum-only field is a legacy metadata artifact or identifies a component not explained in the summary.
Inspect high-resolution surfaces and current codicological scholarship before replacing the conflict with a normalized support statement.
Continue to cite James's paper-with-vellum-guards statement separately from the MODS fields.

open_questions.question: What unpublished or archival material survives from Kock's promised introduction, notes, glossaries, and other critical apparatus?
next_steps: Consult EETS and Kock archival records, correspondence, proofs, and editorial papers.
Distinguish the line-level footnotes and marginal summaries printed in Parts I-II from the broader apparatus Ackerman says was lacking.
Record any surviving materials as separate archival entities rather than treating the 1932 text installment as a complete critical edition.

open_questions.question: Can the 1420s London composition claim be narrowed or reconciled more securely with the traditional circa-1450 date and Parker's circa-1450-75 witness date?
next_steps: Review current evidence for Henry Barton's patronage and death date, Lovelich's civic records, and linguistic chronology.
Keep work composition, translator activity, scribal copying, and manuscript production as separate dated events.
Do not transfer the witness's date or Essex scribal profile to the work without independent evidence.

open_questions.question: Can an unrestricted lawful reproduction of Kock Part III be obtained, and was its 1932 United States copyright secured and renewed?
next_steps: Search renewal records and EETS/Oxford rights files for Original Series 185.
Use a library copy or the Internet Archive controlled-lending item for private comparison while respecting access terms.
Recheck public-domain status after 1 January 2028 if no earlier basis is established, and cache a complete authorized public source if one appears.
